message.content:
Yeah dude, let’s do this.

I’ll give you a loose “script” you can tweak. I’ll keep it casual, friendly, a bit jokey, light swearing but not like… career-ending. You can pick and choose lines that feel like you.

---

### 1. Kicking it off / setting the vibe

“Hey, thanks for making time to chat. I’ve been thinking about this review a bit, so I came prepared with some stuff I’m proud of and some areas I want to level up in.”

If you want more casual:
> “Alright, let’s do this performance review thing. I actually did my homework, so I’m not just winging it… for once.”

---

### 2. Talking about what you did well (without sounding cocky)

Pick a few examples and plug them in:

**Option A (chill confident):**
> “So, in terms of what went well this cycle, I’m pretty happy with:
> - **[Project 1]** – I helped with [what you did], which led to [result, metric, or impact]. That one felt like a win.
> - **[Project 2]** – I stepped in on [problem or situation], and we ended up [saving time/money, unblocking team, making user happy, etc.].
> - **Day-to-day stuff** – I’ve been trying to be the ‘reliable person’ on the team, so when shit hits the fan, people know I’ll jump in and help.”

**Option B (more jokey):**
> “I feel like my biggest wins were:
> - **[Project 1]** – That thing was a bit of a mess at first, but I helped turn it into something we’re not embarrassed to ship.
> - **[Project 2]** – I basically became the ‘[X topic] person’ and helped everyone not lose their minds over it.
> - In general, I’ve tried to be the ‘no drama, just get it done’ person on the team.”

---

### 3. Owning your growth areas (without shitting on yourself)

**Option A (honest, balanced):**
> “Stuff I know I can do better:
> - **[Area 1]** – I’ve noticed I can be [e.g., slow to give updates / too in the weeds / hesitant to push back]. I want to get better at [concrete behavior].
> - **[Area 2]** – When things get busy, I sometimes [e.g., prioritize speed over documentation / forget to loop people in]. I’m working on building better habits there.”

**Option B (casual, self-aware):**
> “I’m also not pretending I’m perfect:
> - I know I can improve on **[area]** – I tend to [thing you do], and I want to get more intentional about [better behavior].
> - And when shit gets hectic, I sometimes [small flaw]. I’m trying to catch myself earlier instead of cleaning it up after.”

---

### 4. Asking for feedback (and not making it awkward)

> “From your side, where do you see my biggest strengths? Like, what should I double down on?”
>
> “And what are the 1–2 things that, if I got better at them, would make you go ‘damn, that’s a big upgrade’?”

If you want a slightly spicier version:
> “If you had to be brutally honest: what am I underestimating about myself, and what am I overestimating?”

---

### 5. Talking about growth / career goals

> “Looking forward, I’d really like to grow more in **[direction: leadership, ownership, technical depth, visibility, etc.]**.
>
> “I’d love your help thinking about:
> - What kind of projects I should be taking on.
> - What ‘next level me’ looks like in this role.
> - And what I’d need to show consistently for you to say, ‘Yep, they’re ready for the next step.’”

More casual:
> “Big picture, I don’t want to just be doing the same shit on repeat. I want to level up in **[area]**. What would you want to see from me over the next 6–12 months to say, ‘Okay, they’re clearly operating at the next level’?”

---

### 6. Talking about support you need (without sounding needy)

> “One thing that would help me a lot is **[type of support: more context, clearer priorities, chances to lead a project, etc.]**.
>
> “If you see opportunities where I can take more ownership or visibility, I’m game—even if there’s a bit
